topic: An employee is asked to train their replacement
raw_output:
[story 1]

The invitation landed in my inbox like an unwelcome tax form. "Sarah, we need you to onboard Mark. He'll be taking over the client management portfolio next week." I reread the email twice, my thumb hovering over the reply button before settling on a slow, deliberate click. A faint tightening began in my chest, a familiar knot of resentment forming just beneath my ribs. Mark. Another fresh face, another opportunity to watch someone else fumble through the routine I had perfected over three years. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of the wheels against the polished floor sounding offensively loud in the quiet office. When Mark finally showed up, fidgeting with his notepad, I offered a curt nod. "Alright," I said, my voice flat, devoid of any warmth. "Let's get started." Every instruction I gave was clipped, every explanation delivered with the weary patience of someone forced to babysit a novice. I made sure my tone suggested that this was less a mentorship and more an administrative chore I was reluctantly completing.

[story 2]

"It’s your turn now, Alex," my manager announced, leaning back in his ergonomic chair with an air of practiced optimism. I felt a slow burn ignite behind my eyes. I looked at Mark, who was practically vibrating with nervous energy, and I consciously forced my expression into something neutral, something utterly uninvested. *Here we go again,* I thought, a sour taste rising in my mouth. I began walking him through the CRM system, my movements precise, almost mechanical. I pointed to screens, narrated the clicks, and kept my gaze fixed just over his shoulder, never meeting his eyes. When he inevitably asked a question I had already answered in the training manual, I paused, letting a long silence stretch between us. "That’s covered in Module C, Mark," I stated, my tone carrying a distinct lack of enthusiasm, as if explaining basic arithmetic to a toddler. I felt a distinct urge to check my watch, eager for the ordeal to be over so I could return to tasks that actually required my attention.

[story 3]

I received the assignment with a sigh that felt too heavy for the small space of my cubicle. Training the new hire. The thought immediately brought a wave of irritation. I watched Mark arrive, carrying a stack of binders that looked far too large for his frame. He offered a tentative smile, a gesture that seemed entirely misplaced in this professional setting. I resisted the urge to roll my eyes, instead focusing intently on the task at hand. "Start with the intake process," I instructed, my voice deliberately low and even. I moved through the procedures with a speed that bordered on aggressive, ensuring every step was covered, not because I cared about his success, but because I couldn't tolerate inefficiency. When Mark struggled with a specific data entry field, I sighed audibly, a sound laced with palpable exasperation. "Look closer," I murmured, leaning in just enough to make the proximity uncomfortable. "It's right there. Try again." The effort of maintaining this level of controlled coolness was exhausting, leaving me feeling drained and profoundly annoyed by the necessity of the situation.
